text: College students with classes till 3 PM should use the 4–7 PM golden window for deep work: assignments, projects, or technical subjects. Evenings post-dinner are best for lighter revision and reading.
For mid-semester and end-semester exams, start subject-wise revision 3 weeks in advance. Dedicate 2 days per subject minimum. Avoid last-minute cramming which destroys conceptual understanding.
LeetCode practice should be scheduled daily: 1 easy problem in the morning to warm up, 1 medium problem post-lunch. Save hard problems for weekends when you have longer uninterrupted focus blocks.
For DBMS, OS, CN, and other CS core subjects, study theory Monday–Wednesday and solve past year university questions Thursday–Friday. This theory-to-practice cycle maximises exam scores.
Minor and major project work should have dedicated slots 3 days a week (e.g., Tuesday, Thursday, Saturday). Maintain a GitHub commit streak for projects — it builds your portfolio and keeps momentum.

College students targeting FAANG/top tech placements should invest 2–3 hours daily in DSA + system design. Alternate days: Day 1 arrays/trees/graphs, Day 2 system design case study, Day 3 mixed problems.
For software engineering students, build at least one personal project per semester. Schedule 1 hour on weekday evenings and 3 hours on weekends for project development. Document progress weekly.
Use Cornell notes method for college lectures: note main ideas on the right, cues on the left. Review within 24 hours. This 3-step method reduces re-reading time by 60% before exams.
College students should block 30 minutes daily for internship/job applications: updating resume, writing cold emails, applying to 3–5 companies, and following up. Consistency beats binge applying.
Join or form a study group of 3–5 people for core technical subjects. Weekly 2-hour group sessions where each person teaches one topic is far more effective than solo reading for difficult concepts.
For students with labs and practicals, review the lab manual the evening before each lab session. Prepare viva answers and understand the experiment theory. This saves confusion during lab hours.
Machine learning and AI students should balance theory and code equally. Read a paper or chapter in the morning, implement it in the afternoon. Kaggle competitions on weekends build practical skills.

Maintain a digital second brain (Notion, Obsidian) for college. Organise notes by subject, semester, and topic. Link related concepts. This knowledge base becomes invaluable during placements and interviews.
For open book exams and assignments, create comprehensive formula sheets and concept summaries as you study throughout the semester. Do not scramble to create them right before exams.

College students should schedule one 'no-deadline' creative or exploratory learning hour per week. Explore a new technology, read a technical blog, or watch a conference talk. This prevents burnout and sparks innovation.
For Data Structures, implement every data structure from scratch at least once: linked lists, stacks, queues, trees, graphs, heaps. Understanding implementation beats memorizing APIs for interviews.
Time management for college students: use time-blocking in Google Calendar. Treat study blocks as unmissable appointments. Schedule 15-minute buffer gaps between blocks for mental reset.
Students targeting government exams alongside degree should dedicate morning 5–7 AM for competitive exam prep and use college hours efficiently. Avoid overlap that dilutes both preparations.

Reading technical documentation (React docs, Python docs, AWS docs) for 20 minutes daily builds deep platform knowledge. This differentiates candidates in technical interviews far more than video tutorials.
College students should track their weekly hours using a habit tracker. Aim for 6–8 hours of productive study daily. Review every Sunday: what was skipped? Why? Adjust next week's plan accordingly.
For networking in college: attend 1 tech event, webinar, or hackathon per month. Use LinkedIn actively — post about your projects and learnings. 70% of tech jobs are filled through referrals.
Avoid all-nighters before exams. A rested brain outperforms a cramming exhausted one. Sleep 7–8 hours before exam day. Use the morning of the exam for a 30-minute calm revision of key formulas only.
